title: Jump Game
code: if (i > farthest) return false;          // can't even reach i

frames.caption: Initialize farthest = 0 — standing on index 0 you can reach at least index 0. Goal is index 4.
frames.note: We track exactly one number, `farthest`: the rightmost index reachable using any sequence of jumps seen so far. The loop invariant is that every index in [0, farthest] is reachable. Maintaining just this scalar (instead of a per-index DP table) is what turns reachability from an O(n²) problem into a single O(n) pass with O(1) space.

frames.caption: i = 0: 0 <= farthest = 0, so index 0 is reachable. Stand here and see how far we can jump.
frames.note: The guard `i > farthest` is the whole correctness test: `farthest` is the furthest index any prior jump could land on, so if i is still within it, index i is genuinely reachable and worth standing on. The greedy insight (a "stays ahead" argument) is that we never need to remember WHICH jumps got us here — only that we CAN be here. That single summary makes the naive O(n²) DP `reach[i]` unnecessary and collapses the whole problem to one O(1)-state scalar.

frames.caption: farthest = max(0, 0 + 2) = max(0, 2) = 2. Reach now covers indices 0..2.
frames.note: From index i we may jump up to nums[i] steps, so the new candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the MAX so the frontier never shrinks — a later index with a tiny jump can never undo the long jump an earlier index unlocked. Because `farthest` only ever grows, one left-to-right sweep suffices: there is no backtracking and no table, just a running high-water mark of reachability.

frames.caption: i = 1: 1 <= farthest = 2, so index 1 is reachable. Stand here and see how far we can jump.
frames.note: The guard `i > farthest` is the whole correctness test: `farthest` is the furthest index any prior jump could land on, so if i is still within it, index i is genuinely reachable and worth standing on. frames.caption: farthest = max(4, 4 + 4) = max(4, 8) = 8. Reach now covers indices 0..4.
frames.note: From index i we may jump up to nums[i] steps, so the new candidate reach is i + nums[i]. We keep the MAX so the frontier never shrinks — a later index with a tiny jump can never undo the long jump an earlier index unlocked. Because `farthest` only ever grows, one left-to-right sweep suffices: there is no backtracking and no table, just a running high-water mark of reachability.

frames.caption: Loop finished without ever being stranded, and farthest = 8 >= goal = 4. The last index is reachable — return true.
frames.note: Reaching the end of the loop means index i never outran `farthest`, so every index — including the last — was reachable. This is the greedy "stays ahead" guarantee paying off: tracking only the furthest reach was a sufficient summary of all jump choices, and it beat the O(n²) DP outright at O(n) time and O(1) space.
